coredata.dc:description: Given the ongoing proliferation of bacterial resistance, particularly the rise of “superbugs”, it is imperative to create novel, effective, non-toxic, and non-resistant antibacterial materials and technologies for use in medical settings. Carbon dots derived from dopamine hydrochloride and citric acid (DA-CDs) were effectively produced through a two-step method, displaying photodynamic therapy (PDT) characteristics with low-drug resistance. Only a concentration of 0.125 μg mL−1 of DA-CDs can effectively break down established S. aureus biofilms when exposed to blue light (450–500 nm), resulting in a biofilm destruction rate of 96.2 %. And after 28 passages, the sensitivity of S. aureus and E. coli to DA-CDs when irradiated with blue light stays the same. The ultra-high negative zeta potential of DA-CDs (-35 mV) and excess intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) induced by DA-CDs accounted for their antibacterial and antibiofilm effects. Furthermore, DA-CDs exhibit outstanding biocompatibility without causing harm to red blood cells (RBCs), 3T3 Cells, bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). The DA-CDs from this study can effectively kill harmful bacteria and destroy established biofilms using PDT, providing a new alternative to antibiotics and a valuable tool for exploring new treatments for biofilm-related infections.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from dopamine for potent photodynamic bactericidal and antibiofilm application with biocompatibility and low bacterial resistance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the ongoing proliferation of bacterial resistance, particularly the rise of “superbugs”, it is imperative to create novel, effective, non-toxic, and non-resistant antibacterial materials and technologies for use in medical settings. Carbon dots derived from dopamine hydrochloride and citric acid (DA-CDs) were effectively produced through a two-step method, displaying photodynamic therapy (PDT) characteristics with low-drug resistance. Only a concentration of 0.125 μg mL−1 of DA-CDs can effectively break down established S. aureus biofilms when exposed to blue light (450–500 nm), resulting in a biofilm destruction rate of 96.2 %. And after 28 passages, the sensitivity of S. aureus and E. coli to DA-CDs when irradiated with blue light stays the same. The ultra-high negative zeta potential of DA-CDs (-35 mV) and excess intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) induced by DA-CDs accounted for their antibacterial and antibiofilm effects. Furthermore, DA-CDs exhibit outstanding biocompatibility without causing harm to red blood cells (RBCs), 3T3 Cells, bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). The DA-CDs from this study can effectively kill harmful bacteria and destroy established biofilms using PDT, providing a new alternative to antibiotics and a valuable tool for exploring new treatments for biofilm-related infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Infections caused by bacterial biofilms present a danger to the well-being and security of humans [1]. Currently, the primary method of clinical treatment for biofilm infections involves the extensive use of antibiotics [2]. However, the use of a large amount of antibiotics can easily lead to the problem of bacterial resistance [3,4]. Therefore, efficient antibiotic free fungicides are of great significance in combating bacterial infections related diseases [5,6]. Nanomaterials have emerged as a popular area of research in antibacterial materials in the era following antibiotics, thanks to their strong antibacterial properties, resilience against bacteria, and numerous antibacterial locations [7]. And the multiple sterilization mechanisms are not easy to cause bacterial resistance [8]. While several nanomaterials have shown great potential as antibacterial agents, it is important to note that they also come with certain limitations. For instance, nanoparticles made of metal and metal oxides have been found to possess antibacterial and antibiofilm properties [9], nevertheless, they may exhibit cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells, which poses a significant challenge in their biomedical applications [10]. Consequently, the treatment of bacterial infections necessitates the development of nanomaterials that exhibit both bactericidal and anti-biofilm properties, in addition to demonstrating favorable biocompatibility.

Carbon dots, also known as CDs, are a recently developed form of carbon nanomaterials that exhibit excellent biocompatibility and have promising applications in the field of biomedicine [11–19]. The rich surface states, tunable excited states, and excellent photoelectron transfer properties make CDs possible for sterilization [20]. Its unique photosensitive activity and photodynamic therapy (PDT) have received widespread attention [21]. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) shows potential in fighting against bacteria and biofilm infections by generating ROS that can interfere with biomolecules in bacterial cells, ultimately causing cell death [22,23]. For example, graphene dots generate free radicals under light excitation and exhibit significant phototoxicity, which also confirms the formation of photo induced ROS generated by electron-hole pairs [24]. Our previous findings have shown that CDs made from lysine and arginine exhibit potent antibacterial effects by generating ROS in the presence of light, resulting in the elimination of bacteria and biofilms without causing harm to mammalian cells [25]. The process of photodynamic sterilization can effectively eliminate biofilms from CDs, making it a top alternative to antibiotics.

In this study, dopamine-derived carbon dots (DA-CDs) with photodynamic activities have been developed. In vitro investigations have shown that these CDs exhibit potent antibacterial and antibiofilm properties, along with rapid bactericidal activity. DA-CDs not only have surfaces with strong negative potentials but also contain multiple functional groups modified on their surface. Surface chemistry has a promoting effect on the bactericidal performance of DA-CDs. DA-CDs are excited photodynamically to generate elevated levels of ROS, thereby boosting their effectiveness against bacteria and biofilms. The DA-CDs demonstrate very low cytotoxicity and minimal in vitro toxicity, which is noteworthy. Our results confirm that DA-CDs have the capability to serve as effective antibacterial agents in place of antibiotics for combating biofilms.

As shown in
Scheme 1 , DA-CDs were prepared from CA and DA using a straightforward two-step process. The first step was to obtain CDs from CA (CA-CDs). A gram of calcium was placed directly into a covered ceramic dish and heated at 180 °C for three hours in a furnace. Once the furnace temperature had cooled to room temperature on its own, the remaining material was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water. After sonication for 30 minutes, the CA-CDs mixture was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for another 30 minutes at room temperature. After dialyzing the supernatant in DI water for 24 hours using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of 500 Da, the water was replaced every hour. After 48 hours of vacuum freeze-drying, the CA-CDs powder was stored in cold storage at 4 °C. The second step is to synthesis DA-CDs from CA-CDs and DA by hydrothermal method. A common method for creating the compound involves combining 500 mg of CA-CDs with 500 mg of DA in a 100 mL autoclave lined with Teflon. A volume of 50 mL of Millipore water was added, then tightly sealed and placed in a furnace at a temperature of 180 °C for a duration of 8 hours. The autoclave was then permitted to cool down naturally to the temperature of the surrounding environment. After being retrieved from the autoclave, a solution of dark brown crude was spun at 15,000 rpm for 10 minutes to separate the larger particles. A transparent yellow liquid was gathered and passed through a 220 nm ultrafiltration membrane. Then the DA-CDs powders were abstained by freeze-drying after dialysis.

Images of the DA-CDs were captured using a JEM-2100 high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) from JEOL Ltd. in Japan, operating at 200 kV. The samples were examined for phase purity and crystallinity using X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker instrument (D8 ADVANCE, Germany). The range of 2θ was 5°∼80° and the scanning speed was 5°/min. The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption patterns of the DA-CDs were analysed with a Cary 60 spectrophotometer, covering wavelengths from 200 nm to 800 nm. The test liquid in the quartz colorimetric dish was 2 mL and the detection rate is 2 nm min−1. Fluorescence (FL) spectrum was obtained utilizing the F-2700 FL spectrophotometer, with emission spectra ranging from 380 to 800 nm. The excitation wavelength range was 360–500 nm, with intervals of 20 nm. The Nicolet iS10 instrument was used to obtain FTIR spectra of the samples across the wavenumber range from 525 to 4000 cm−1, while the zeta potentials of the DA-CDs were determined with a Malvern ZS90 Zetasizer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI Versa probe 4, Japan) is utilized to analyse the elemental composition of the prepared DA-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of DA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli and S. aureus were choose as model bacteria in the measurement of DA-CDs’ antibacterial activity [26]. After incubating the bacteria overnight at 37 °C in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth, they were then rotated at 3500 g for 10 minutes at 20 °C, washed twice with PBS, and finally diluted to a concentration of 105 to 106 CFU mL−1. The concentration of DA-CDs in LB broth was serially diluted by a factor of two, yielding concentrations of 1000, 500, 250, 125, 62.5, 31.25 and 15.625 µg mL−1, or alternatively, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.0625 and 0.03125 µg mL−1. Following this, 10 μL of a bacterial solution with a concentration of 105 to 106 CFU mL−1 was added to per suspension. The experiment used bacteria in LB medium as the control, and LB medium without DA-CDs as the blank. The mixtures were placed in an incubator set at 37 °C and stirred at a rate of 200 rpm. After incubating for 24 hours, each test tube was visually inspected for cloudiness. The MIC values were determined as the point at which no cloudiness could be seen compared to the control. Each data was iterated 3 times in order to acquire the average value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial kinetic of DA-CDs with or without blue light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria killing by DA-CDs was assessed using established methods for measuring kinetics [27]. E. coli and S. aureus were grown in LB medium until they reached mid-log phase to produce suspensions with 106 and 107 CFU mL−1, respectively. The bacterial suspensions were then cultured with 100 μg mL−1 of dopamine-coated carbon dots (DA-CDs) for various durations ranging from 0 to 4 hours. The blend was diluted with disinfectant PBS (pH 7.4) at designated time intervals (0, 10, 30, 60, 120, 180, and 240 minutes), then subjected to 10-fold serial dilutions and plated on LB solid plates. The bacterial colonies that grown on every plate were counted and drew based on the time they were incubated for 18 hours at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation of bacteria using scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
ce:section.ce:para: Suspensions of bacteria (1.0 × 108 CFU mL−1 for S. aureus and 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 for E. coli) and DA-CDs (100 μg mL−1) were mixed in a 6-well plate and placed on a shaker at 37 °C for 30 minutes with or without blue light exposure. After incubation, the bacterial cells were fixed using 2 mL of 2.5 % glutaraldehyde. The surplus glutaraldehyde was eliminated, and the bacteria that had been treated were rinsed with PBS on three occasions. After being treated, the bacteria were dried by exposing them to a series of ethanol solutions with concentrations varying from 30 % to 100 %. After critical point drying with CO2 and gold spraying, the bacterial cell morphology was analysed with SEM [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental study on bacterial resistance to DA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC values of rifampicin and DA-CDs against S. aureus and E. coli were initially determined using a previously described method [26]. Bacteria from a 96-well plate, having a concentration that is half of the MIC, were utilized to produce a bacterial dilution (around 107 CFU mL−1) for the following MIC evaluation. After incubating bacterial suspensions with rifampicin and various DA-CDs, new MIC values were established following a 24-hour period at 37°C. This experimental procedure was repeated daily for a total of 28 passages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of ROS levels in S. aureus and E. coli after exposure to DA-CDs with or without blue light
ce:section.ce:para: Reference showed that ROS generation was observed by measuring the transformation of DCFH-DA into non-fluorescent 2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF) [25]. This conversion is facilitated by cellular esterases, with DCF subsequently being oxidized by ROS to form fluorescent DCF [29]. Analysis using a FL spectrometer excited at 488 nm revealed a peak emission of DCF at 525 nm [30]. The level of ROS present was found to be directly correlated with the intensity of FL exhibited by DCF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteria release cytoplasmic β-galactosidase after treatment with DA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli bacteria in suspensions containing 106∼107 CFU mL−1 were treated with solutions of 100 μg mL−1DA-CDs (pH7.4). The treatment was conducted at 37 °C with shaking for a duration of 30 minutes. Positive and negative controls were prepared using lysed E. coli and untreated E. coli, respectively. Afterward, the bacteria suspensions were spun at a relative centrifugal force of 3500 g for 10 minutes, and the resulting liquids were then mixed with o-Nitrophenyl β-D-galactopyranoside (ONPG, 25 mg mL−1) and left to incubate for two hours at room temperature. The absorbance of Ortho-nitrophenol (ONP) was determined using UV-Vis spectroscopy by measuring the OD420 value [31].

TTC, also known as 2, 3, 5-Triphenyltetrazolium chloride, interacts with succinic dehydrogenase found in the mitochondria of viable cells, resulting in the formation of red methylene, a marker for cell viability. The survival rate of bacteria is directly correlated with the quantity of red or pink triphenylformazan present in the medium when TTC serves as an electron acceptor. In our previously published report, we evaluated the intracellular dehydrogenase activity through the TTC-dehydrogenase activity assay [31]. In short, test tubes were filled with 900 μL of PBS solution (with a pH of 7.4) mixed with different levels of DA-CDs, along with 100 μL of a new bacterial suspension (1 × 107 CFU mL−1). After a 30 minutes incubation period, 1 mL of a 4 % TTC-glucose mixture was added to test tubes wrapped in tin foil and placed in a shaking incubator at 37 °C [25]. DA-CDs were not present in the experiment as a control measure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal violet (CV) experiment to detect the activity of DA-CDs in destroying biofilms
ce:section.ce:para:
S. aureus was incubated overnight in LB medium to generate a fresh bacterial suspension, which was then added to a sterile 96-well plate containing 200 μL of trypsinogen soy broth medium (TSB) inoculated with 10 μL of 105 CFU mL−1 bacteria per well. The plate was subsequently placed in a constant temperature and humidity incubator for 24 hours, during which time S. aureus proliferated and formed a dense white biofilm at the well bottom. Following cultivation, the liquid TSB was gently removed and the biofilm was rinsed 3 times with pH7.4 PBS. Subsequently, 200 μL of TSB solution was added to every well containing DA-CDs, followed by a 24-hour incubation period in the incubator with or without blue light irradiation. Planktonic bacteria and DA-CDs were then removed from the supernatant and rinsed with PBS for 3 times. The damaged biofilm was dehumidify in a 60 °C oven and fixed with methanol for fifteen minutes. Then, the methanol was cast away and the biofilm was completely dried at 60 °C before staining the nucleic acid of live bacteria with 0.1 % CV for fifteen minutes. Excess CV was then removed and the biofilm was rinsed with PBS three times. After drying at 60 °C, biofilms were subjected to decolourization using 200 μL of 95 % ethanol. The decolourization process was conducted on a shaking table at 37 °C for 30 minutes. Subsequently, the absorption value of the mixed solution was measured at 570 nm using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader. The biofilm that did not undergo treatment with DA-CDs as a control group, with its destruction rate set at 0%. Absorbance tests were repeated three times for each well to get an average data, and the damage rate of each DA-CDs to the biofilm was calculated using a specific formula [27].

The 3T3 cell, BMSCs and HUVECs was utilized as the experimental model to assess the cytotoxic effects of DA-CDs using the CCK-8 assay [25]. At first, cells were placed in 96-well plates with a concentration of 1 × 104 cells per well. Following this, different amounts of DA-CDs (ranging between 1 and 4 mg mL−1) were added to the wells and left to incubate at 37 °C for 24 hours while exposed to blue light. After being exposed, the wells were rinsed three times with PBS buffer to eliminate any remaining DA-CDs. Afterwards, each well received 10 μL of CCK-8 reagent and 100 μL of new Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), followed by an additional 2-hour incubation at 37 °C without exposure to light. Subsequent OD450 measurements were conducted using a microplate reader from Perkin-Elmer (Victor III) to determine the counts of viable cells. A positive control was executed with untreated cells, yielding a cell survival rate of 100 %. Mean values were calculated by repeating the experiment thrice. The OD450 measurements were calculated after 1 days, 2 days and 3 days using the procedure described earlier [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of cell viability in 3T3 cells exposed to DA-CDs using live/dead staining
ce:section.ce:para: At first, a new mixture of DMEM with 10 % serum and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin was made. Different amounts of DA-CDs, varying between 1 and 4 mg mL−1, were mixed with 200 μL of cell culture medium and 10,000 cells in separate wells of a 96-well plate. Following a 24-hour period of exposure to blue light, the liquid was removed and the wells were washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4) to eliminate any remaining liquid. Next, 100 μL of Live/Dead dye solution was added to each well and left to incubate at room temperature for fifteen minutes in a darkened area. Observation of cell structure and acquisition of FL images were performed with an inverted FL microscope [25]. The control group consisted of cells that did not receive treatment with CDs.

The hemolysis caused by DA-CDs was in accordance with a process that had been previously documented [33]. New blood specimens from individuals in good health were collected from their veins and placed in tubes with EDTA, then spun at an RCF of 3000 g for 10 minutes within 30 minutes to separate the serum. RBCs were obtained following the rules of the institution and then rinsed twice with disinfectant PBS until the liquid above them was no longer cloudy. DA-CDs in PBS (500 µL) were combined with RBCs stock suspension (500 µL) in each vial at concentrations varying from 1 to 4 mg mL−1. After incubating for 1 hour at 37 °C, the vials were rotated for 10 minutes at 1000 g. Hemolytic performance was assessed by measuring the absorbance of hemoglobin in supernatant at 576 nm (OD576CDs). Disinfectant PBS was utilized as the control for measuring 0% haemolysis (OD576PBS), while the addition of the RBCs suspension to ultrapure water served as the control for 100 % haemolysis (OD576H2O). A formula was employed to determine the hemolytic activity.

Mean ± SD represented all values, which were obtained from a minimum of three free-running tests. The significance of the data was evaluated using the double-tailed heteroscedasticity t-test, as calculated by the TTEST function in Microsoft Excel. A statistically significant difference between groups was indicated when the P value was less than 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the CDs' ability to inhibit bacterial growth and their time-killing effects
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC test is conducted to assess the antibacterial effectiveness of DA-CDs under different conditions, including exposure to blue light. In Table 1, it is evident that the MIC of the CDs decreases significantly to 0.0625 μg mL−1 when subjected to blue light, as opposed to 31.25 μg mL−1 without light, in the battle against S. aureus. The impact on E. coli inhibition is comparable. Besides, DA-CDs possess higher activity than that of raw dopamine. This indicates that the antibacterial performance has indeed improved significantly after the nanomaterialization of dopamine.

Furthermore, the antibacterial kinetics also demonstrate the effective photodynamic bactericidal activity of DA-CDs ( Fig. 1 A).The graph illustrates the rapid bactericidal effect of DA-CDs within a 10-minute timeframe. Overall, as the culture time of DA-CDs and bacteria increases, the sterilization rate slows down. Under unfavorable conditions (dark), 100 μg mL−1 of DA-CDs only leads to a 1.7 log10 decrease in the amount of S. aureus CFU in 240 minutes, whereas DA-CDs can cause an 8 log10 reduction in just 5 minutes under blue light. In the case of E. coli (Fig. 1B), the graphs also show that the rates of bacteria killing are influenced by the light source. The data above indicates that exposing DA-CDs to blue light can significantly enhance their sterilization effectiveness through photodynamic therapy. Additionally, SEM images were used to analyze the morphology of S. aureus and E. coli after being exposed to DA-CDs under dark or blue light conditions (Fig. 1 C). Upon inspection, it is clear that the raw E. coli and S. aureus have a perfect and unmarked outer surface. And the morphological analysis of bacteria exposed solely to blue light revealed no significant alterations when compared to the control group, with only minor irregularities observed. This suggests that the bacteria remain viable following exposure to blue light alone. This outcome can be attributed to the relatively low power (5 W) and weak intensity of the blue light employed in this study, resulting in minimal damage to the bacteria and negligible impact on their morphology. Following exposure to 100 μg mL−1 DA-CDs in the absence of light for 30 minutes, the cell membrane displayed characteristics like reduction in size, inward curvature, creases, openings, and fractures. Whereas, after being treated with 100 μg mL−1 DA-CDs under blue light irradiation for 30 min, the bacterial cell membrane was completely destroyed, and the internal solution leaked completely, leaving only the body shell. These results indicate that blue light helps enhance the disruption of bacterial cell membrane integrity by DA-CDs, ultimately accelerating bacterial death.

Presently, the issue of bacterial resistance is a significant worry in contemporary healthcare [34]. To evaluate the likelihood of bacteria acquiring resistance to DA-CDs following an extended antibacterial treatment, S. aureus and E. coli are exposed to sublethal doses of antibacterial agents daily, with the antibacterial effectiveness assessed over 28 generations. As depicted in Figs. 1D and 1E, after 28 passages, the sensitivity of S. aureus and E. coli to DA-CDs when exposed to blue light has not changed. However, it was noted that Rifampicin, a readily accessible antibiotic, causes resistance in S. aureus and E. coli following 6 and 10 generations of incubation, respectively. The findings indicate photodynamic antibacterial DA-CDs are less likely to induce drug resistance compared to rifampicin.

The experiments above demonstrate that DA-CDs exhibit photodynamic bactericidal effects and minimal drug resistance. The physicochemical properties of DA-CDs are thoroughly examined and discussed to identify the factors.

Fig. 2A displays HR-TEM images of DA-CDs. The DA-CDs are consistent nanoparticles that are nearly circular in shape and have an average diameter of 4.2 nanometers (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, the lattice gap of 0.215 nm exhibited by the DA-CDs is consistent with the orientation of the (100) surface of graphite, while DA-CDs exhibit plentiful oxygen and nitrogen functional groups embedded in their layers [35]. In Fig. 2C, the XRD pattern of DA-CDs shows wide peaks around 20°. The findings are consistent with the (002) lattice parameter of graphite, displaying turbostratic disorder [36]. In addition, the distinction between sp3 hybridized C and sp2 hybridized C can be made by examining NMR spectra, particularly the 1H (Fig. S1) and 13C (Fig. S2) spectra. sp2 carbon atoms are detectable in the 1H NMR spectrum, whereas in the 13C NMR spectrum, signals within the 20–50 ppm range are indicative of aliphatic carbon (sp3) atoms, signals within the 110–190 ppm range signify sp2 carbon atoms, and signals within the 170–180 ppm range correspond to carboxyl/amide groups. Figure S3 displays the Raman spectra of DA-CDs and provides the results obtained through Raman characterization. Two distinct peaks were detected in the spectrum, corresponding to the D and G bands. The D band, located at 1379 cm−1, is associated with disorder-induced features, indicating the presence of disorder at the edges in the form of sp3 carbons, surface states, or functional groups on the surface of CDs [37]. The G band, observed at 1580 cm−1, is attributed to the stretching of C−C bonds within the sp2 carbon network. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band, denoted as ID/IG, serves as a metric for evaluating the degree of disorder within a material [38]. The measured ID/IG value of 0.82 suggests that the DA-CDs sample contains both crystalline and amorphous regions [39]. Furthermore, UV-Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2D) Indicate that the DA-CDs exhibit dual absorption peaks at approximately 345 nm and 453 nm, which align with the electron transition of the conjugated CC and the functional groups CO and -NH2, respectively [40]. However, no absorption band is observed in the 300–800 nm range for unprocessed DA and CA. Furthermore, it is evident that DA-CDs maintain the absorption of DA at 290 nm, indicating that dopamine remnants are preserved on the surface of DA-CDs. And the DA-CDs solutions in Fig. 2E exhibit a vibrant cyan FL under 365 nm light excitation. The findings indicate that the reaction of CA with DA produced DA-CDs with dopamine residues while undergoing hydrothermal processing. Additionally, Fig. 2F represent contour maps showing the relationship between excitation wavelength and emission wavelength. The corresponding color indicates the emission strength of DA-CDs. We observed the FL of DA-CDs to be dependent on the excitation wavelength, abbreviated as EDF. The presence of surface/edge states on the surface of the DA-CDs could explain the EDF [41,42]. Additionally, the FL emission spectra obtained with excitations ranging from 360 to 500 nm provide further evidence of EDF in DA-CDs, showing peaks with a red-shift and reduced intensity (Fig. 2G). The limited range of characteristics like surface imperfections, energy gap, surface arrangement, and size in the created CDs indicates a single source of FL origins, which are believed to be linked to the various emission patterns [43]. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of DA-CDs in water was determined to be 0.93 % (Fig. S4). The observed low PLQYs of the DA-CDs may be attributed to the abundance of electron-withdrawing carbonyl functional groups, which serve as non-radiative trap centers [44,45]. Additionally, the photoluminescence decay curve was accurately modeled by a single exponential decay function, yielding a photoluminescence lifetime of 1.82 ns (Fig. S5). The mono-exponential decay behavior suggests that the radiative decay process is predominantly pure, with minimal non-radiative contributions [46]. As exhibited in Fig. 2H, following calcination, the peak pattern of the CA FTIR spectrum exhibits a general decrease in intensity and disappearance of sharp peaks, suggesting the occurrence of chemical reactions among CA molecules. Analysis of the CA structure indicates that the primary reaction taking place is esterification (Scheme 1). New peaks at 1170 cm−1 and 1693 cm−1 in the CA-CDs spectrum indicate the presence of stretching vibrations of C-O-C and CO in the ester group. Additionally, the presence of the carboxyl group of CA is confirmed on the surface of the CA-CDs at 1703 cm−1. Subsequently, following co-heating with DA, the surface functional groups of CA-CDs undergo a reaction with hydroxyl and amino groups present in DA, leading to the formation of new functional groups. The spectral analysis reveals a red shift in the CO peak of the carboxyl group on the CA-CDs spectrum, along with the emergence of a C-O-C stretching vibration peak at 1244 cm−1. These observations suggest that the carboxyl group on the surface of CA-CDs undergoes esterification reaction with the hydroxyl group in DA during the high-temperature hydrothermal process, resulting in the formation of ester bonds on the surface of DA-CDs. Furthermore, the FTIR spectra of the DA-CDs display multiple unique peaks related to DA, such as 3100–3500 cm−1 (O-H/N-H), 3038 cm−1 (ArC-H), 2967 (-CH2-), and 1120 cm−1 (C-N), without showing any significant peaks distinctive to CA-CDs. This suggests that the surface of CA-CDs is covered by DA residues. Additionally, a new peak is observed at 1680 cm−1 (CO) and 1434 cm−1 (C-N) when comparing with CA-CDs and DA, indicating the presence of amide bonds. Therefore, the presence of DA residues on the CDs surface is shown through amide reaction and esterification reaction. Furthermore, XPS is conducted to evaluate the elemental composition of the prepared DA-CDs (Fig. S6). The XPS spectrum of DA-CDs displays three significant peaks, specifically the C 1 s peak at 285.0 eV, N 1 s peak at 400.3 eV, and O 1 s peak at 532.9 eV, as illustrated in Fig. S6A. The atomic concentrations of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen are determined to be 68.96 %, 4.23 %, and 26.82 %, respectively. Analysis of the C 1 s spectrum reveal the presence of C-C/CC (284.7 eV), C-N/C-NH2 (285.8 eV), CO (288.1 eV), and π-π* (292.7 eV) bonds (Fig. S6B) [47]. The N 1 s spectrum displayed peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.6 eV, corresponding to N-C and N-H bonds, respectively (Fig. S6C). The O 1 s spectrum depicted in Fig. 2D exhibits two distinct peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.6 eV, separately representing the CO and HO-C/C-O-C functionalities (Fig. S6D). These findings align with the outcomes of FTIR analysis. The zeta potentials of the DA-CDs are determined in solutions with a pH of 7.4 (Fig. 2I) The CDs have a potential of −35 mV as shown by the results of the experiment. This phenomenon could potentially be attributed to the abundance of hydroxyl groups present on the surface of DA-CDs. And we speculate that when DA-CDs come into contact with bacteria, such an ultrahigh negative potential may contribute to the collapse and rupture of bacterial cell membranes, which is the result of electrostatic repulsion. Fig. 1 C may support the above conjecture.

After examining the physical and chemical characteristics of DA-CDs, we predict various ways in which DA-CDs could kill bacteria. To verify our hypothesis, further analysis will be conducted through experimental data on specific sterilization mechanisms.

The FL emitted by DCF is utilized for the indirect assessment of the intracellular ROS generated by the DA-CDs at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1, with or without exposure to blue light. As shown in Fig. 3 A, even in dark environments, DA-CDs are present in S. aureus produce more ROS than that of the H2O2 group. By comparison, DA-CDs significantly increase the production of ROS in S. aureus when exposed to blue light. Comparable results are obtained utilizing a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Fig. S7). Similarly, these phenomena also occur in E. coli (Fig. 3B). It is a logical outcome that the absorbed energy from blue light can stimulate electrons on functional groups like CO, CC, -OH, and -NH2 on the surface of DA-CDs to go through transitions (Fig. 2D), resulting in electron-hole pairs [48]. This will lead to the generation of ROS, thereby enhancing antibacterial activity.

The integrity of the bacterial membrane was assessed using the ONPG test, which measured the release of β-galactosidase from lysed S. aureus and E. coli after being exposed to DA-CDs [49]. As shown in Fig. 3 C, the absorbance at 420 nm is respectively 0.22 and 0.43 after S. aureus treated with DA-CDs under dark or blue light environment for 30 min, which indicating high membrane damage efficiency under blue light irradiation. The absorbance is separately 0.24 and 0.42 for E. coli with or without blue light irradiation (Fig. 3D). Hence, DA-CDs have the ability to enter the plasma membrane and cause leakage of cytoplasm, in line with findings from SEM observations (Fig. 1 C). This phenomenon could be contributed to the ultrahigh surface negative potential of DA-CDs (Fig. 2I), which can make bacteria to form concavity and cracks through electrostatic repulsion force.

Additionally, the activity of E. coli and S. aureus was significantly reduced following exposure to only 0.256 μg mL−1 DA-CDs under blue light for 30 minutes (Fig. 3E, Fig. S8). However, in dark conditions, the level of DA-CDs that can inhibit bacterial activity can be as high as 128 μg mL−1. Exposure to blue light is crucial for augmenting the inhibition of bacterial activity by DA-CDs, which would impact the system of pyridine-nucleoside structural enzyme within the bacterial respiratory chain [50,51].

Overall, the DA-CDs exhibiting oxidase-like properties can efficiently produce ROS when exposed to light. These ROS then react with the bacterial cell walls and/or membranes, causing peroxidation and altering the fluidity and permeability of the membranes. This ultimately results in modifications to the cellular structures and functions. Additionally, the negatively-charged surface of DA-CDs can effectively repel negatively-charged bacteria, potentially contributing to the enhanced antibacterial properties of DA-CDs. Additionally, studies have shown that DA-CDs can block the system of pyridine nucleotide structural enzyme within the bacterial respiratory chain [50–52]. This phenomenon may also be due to ROS. DA-CDs exhibit exceptional photodynamic antibacterial efficacy due to the synergistic effects of electrostatic repulsion and ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the disruption of biofilms by DA-CDs using blue light involves both quantitative and qualitative methods
ce:section.ce:para: From above results, with the help of blue light, the DA-CDs demonstrate multi-modes in activity of anti-planktonic bacteria. Following this, we will assess the antibiofilm effectiveness of the DA-CDs through quantitative and qualitative methods. To begin with, as illustrated in Fig. 4 A and Fig. S9, the images of bacterial biofilms (S. aureus and E. coli) dyed with crystal violet (CV) are easily seen to evaluate the impact of DA-CDs on mature S. aureus and E.coli biofilm under dark and blue light conditions. It displays DA-CDs have an unsatisfactory effect on the elimination of S. aureus and E. coli biofilm in dark, while DA-CDs can potently remove S. aureus and E. coli biofilm under blue light irradiation. And Fig. 4B shows that the rate of degradation is influenced by the amount of DA-CDs present in the absence of light. The higher the concentration, the greater the damage rate. When the doses of the DA-CDs reach 256 μg mL−1, the destruction ratios are 64.1 %. Importantly, with the presence of blue light, DA-CDs has obviously stronger ability to destroy biofilm than that of under darkness. At a concentration of only 0.125 μg mL−1, the DA-CDs can effectively destroy S. aureus biofilms with blue light, achieving a destruction ratio of 96.2 %. Cell viability within the biofilm is quantitatively determined using the spread plate method (Fig. 4 C). Cells cultured in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) have a survival rate of 100 %. The data from Fig. 4 C shows that the survival rate of S. aureus when exposed to DA-CDs at a concentration of 0.125 μg mL−1 is nearly 100 % without light, but decreases to just 5.2 % under blue light exposure. The presence of colonies of diluted S. aureus within the treated mature biofilm is illustrated in Fig. 4D, which is consistent with the results of CV staining analysis. The aforementioned experimental data on antibiofilm show that blue light is essential in eliminating biofilms with DA-CDs. This also confirms that photodynamic activity is the main reason for combating biofilms through sterilization, as DA-CDs have multiple antibacterial mechanisms, the most important of which is ROS [53].

As mentioned above, DA-CDs show significant antibacterial properties with multiple antibacterial modes. An ideal material with antibacterial properties should possess both effective antibacterial qualities and excellent biocompatibility. 3T3 cell viability is determined by the CCK-8 test with different levels of DA-CDs, either in darkness or under light exposure. As shown in Fig. 5 A, following exposure to DA-CDs without light, the harmful impact is low, and cell viability stays high at over 95 % even at 4 mg mL−1. And at a concentration of 4 mg mL−1 of DA-CDs, the viability of cells following exposure to blue light is 93 %. Furthermore, the 3T3 cells are subjected to LIVE/DEAD staining to evaluate the cytotoxic impact of DA-CDs when exposed to blue light (Fig. 5B). DA-CDs exhibited hardly decrease in dead cells at a concentration of 4 mg mL−1 when compared to the control group. And as illustrated in Fig. S10, DA-CDs demonstrate an effect comparable to that observed in BMSCs and HUVECs, akin to the response seen in 3T3 cells, without exhibiting significant toxicity. These results indicated that DA-CDs has good biocompatibility and negligible photo-toxicity.

Furthermore, a hemolysis test is conducted in human blood without fibrin to determine the compatibility of DA-CDs with RBCs in a biological environment under blue light (Fig. 5 C). Following exposure to varying concentrations of CDs (1–4 mg mL−1) for 1 hour, both absorbance analysis of supernatants and images of the treated RBCs were obtained. Remarkably, the liquid above the RBCs stayed clear even when the concentration reached 4 mg mL−1, suggesting that there was no hemolysis. This outcome demonstrates the strong preference of DA-CDs for bacteria compared to RBCs. Therefore, this outcome suggests that DA-CDs are unlikely to cause hemolysis when used in photodynamic antibacterial therapy.

To summarize, we created nano-antibiotics called DA-CDs by subjecting citric acid and dopamine to pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment. These DA-CDs showed excellent blue light photodynamic bactericidal and antibiofilm activities with unlikelihood of forming bacterial resistance. With the prescece of blue light, the surface chemisty of DA-CDs and intracellular ROS induced by DA-CDs are associated with their antibacterial modes, which lead to bacterial membrane rupture and mature biofilm elimination. Additionally, the cytotoxicity evaluation conducted in a laboratory setting indicated that DA-CDs did not harm 3T3 cells and RBCs, even when exposed to concentrations up to 4 mg mL−1. In brief, the favorable properties of selective toxicity toward only bacteria cells can be the candidate materials to replace antibiotics. Additionally, the DA-CDs have the potential to be utilized for treating wound infections, creating different antibacterial coatings, and interacting with biological interfaces.
